It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])

Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. {{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery> <!-- Deleted image removed: Image:Nanzenji3.jpg|A high rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Nanzen-ji]]. Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

"Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery> <!-- Deleted image removed: Image:Nanzenji3.jpg|A high rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Nanzen-ji]]. Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

"gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.

A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>

Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>

{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]][[Category:Japanese words and phrases]]}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery> <!-- Deleted image removed: Image:Nanzenji3.jpg|A high rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Nanzen-ji]]. Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery> <!-- Deleted image removed: Image:Nanzenji3.jpg|A high rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Nanzen-ji]]. Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}
]]
]]
]]
]]

===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery> <!-- Deleted image removed: Image:Nanzenji3.jpg|A high rank, five-bay ''sanmon'' at [[Nanzen-ji]]. Note the ''sanrō''. --> Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Gates in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Sanmon]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:11]] }} [[en:Sanmon]] [[sn:Sanmon]]

===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

revision.comment: Text replacement - "rank" to "Rank"
revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=[[:Category:kk]]}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}} {{draft categories|
}}

It is a high Rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])

revision.comment: Bot: mark page as missing translated-page template
revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>

Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Pages and Categories from Wikipedia
revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[butsuden]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[:en:Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])

{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Kairō|Kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[Kamakura, Kanagawa|Kamakura]], [[Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[:en:Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[Ken (architecture)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Kairō|Kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[Ken (architecture)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Kyoto, Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[:en:Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's [[National Treasures of Japan|National Treasure]])]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-[[:en:Ken (unit)|bay]] ''[[nijūmon]]'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''[[sōmon]]'' (outer gate) and the ''[[:en:Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)]]'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the [[Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|main hall]]). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''[[:en:Kairō|Kairō]]''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the [[Muromachi period]], being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in [[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]] is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''[[:en:Ken (unit)|ken]]''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''[[satori]]''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the [[Niō]], wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in [[Osaka]] </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of [[Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|Kōmyō-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' in [[:en:Kamakura|Kamakura]], [[:en:Kanagawa Prefecture|Kanagawa Prefecture]]. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[:en:Kyoto|Kyoto]]) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ([[Minobu, Yamanashi|Minobu]])
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[:en:Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}
{{draft categories| [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Gates in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]]
}}

;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[Nara, Nara|Nara]]) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ([[:en:Ikaruga, Nara|Ikaruga]]) *[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Tōshō-gū]]'s ''yomeimon'' ([[Nikkō, Tochigi|Nikkō]])
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
