Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay}} ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon}}'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=main hall}}). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|WD=Q7884494|lt=Kairō}}''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''{{ill|Satori|en|Satori|WD=Q584580|lt=Satori}}''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}}, wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of {{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|WD=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji}}'s ''sanmon'' in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Minobu, Yamanashi|en|Minobu, Yamanashi|WD=Q1204435|lt=Minobu}})
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|WD=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga}}) *{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Tōshō-gū}}'s ''yomeimon'' ({{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}})
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>

Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} </gallery>

It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q2975604]] Wikidata name: sanmon [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:05, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure}})]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay}} ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon}}'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon}}'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=main hall}}). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|WD=Q7884494|lt=Kairō}}''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period}}, being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}} is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken}}''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''{{ill|Satori|en|Satori|WD=Q584580|lt=Satori}}''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)}}, wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka}} </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of {{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|WD=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji}}'s ''sanmon'' in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Minobu, Yamanashi|en|Minobu, Yamanashi|WD=Q1204435|lt=Minobu}})
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara}}) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|WD=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga}}) *{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Tōshō-gū}}'s ''yomeimon'' ({{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō}})
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}
[[en:Sanmon]]

Note the ''sanrō''. --> <!--enwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|San-mon}} [[es:Sanmon]] [[fr:Sanmon]] [[it:Sanmon]] [[ja:三門]] [[ms:Sanmon]] [[pt:Sanmon]] [[ru:Саммон]] [[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] {{wikidata link|Q2975604}} [[Category:Gates in Japan]] [[Category:Japanese Buddhist architecture]] [[Category:Japanese words and phrases]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:San-mon]][[en:Sanmon]][[es:Sanmon]][[fr:Sanmon]][[it:Sanmon]][[ja:三門]][[ms:Sanmon]][[pt:Sanmon]][[ru:Саммон]][[uk:Головні ворота (буддизм)]]
{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay|1=Ken (unit)}} ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon|1=Nijūmon}}'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon|1=Sōmon}}'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=main hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|WD=Q7884494|lt=Kairō|1=Kairō}}''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''{{ill|Satori|en|Satori|WD=Q584580|lt=Satori|1=Satori}}''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}, wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of {{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|WD=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji|1=Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)}}'s ''sanmon'' in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Minobu, Yamanashi|en|Minobu, Yamanashi|WD=Q1204435|lt=Minobu|1=Minobu, Yamanashi}})
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|WD=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}) *{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}'s ''yomeimon'' ({{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}})
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.
==Notes== <references/>
==References== *{{cite journal |last = Fowler |first = Sherry |year = 2007 |title = Review of "Daitokuji: The Visual Cultures of a Zen Monastery" by Gregory P. A. Levine |journal = Japanese Journal of Religious Studies |issue = 34/2 |url = http://www.nanzan-u.ac.jp/SHUBUNKEN/publications/jjrs/pdf/774.pdf }}{{dead link|date=May 2018 |bot=InternetArchiveBot |fix-attempted=yes }} * "Sanmon" from the [http://www.aisf.or.jp/~jaanus/ Japanese Art Net User System (JAANUS)] online dictionary accessed on May 2, 2009 *''Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten'' (岩波日本史辞典), CD-Rom Version. Iwanami Shoten, 1999-2001. {{Commons Category|Sanmon}} {{Japanese architectural elements}} {{Buddhist temples in Japan}}

"Hall of Buddha", i.e. the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=main hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|WD=Q7884494|lt=Kairō|1=Kairō}}''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''{{ill|Satori|en|Satori|WD=Q584580|lt=Satori|1=Satori}}''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}, wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of {{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|WD=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji|1=Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)}}'s ''sanmon'' in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Minobu, Yamanashi|en|Minobu, Yamanashi|WD=Q1204435|lt=Minobu|1=Minobu, Yamanashi}})
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|WD=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}) *{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}'s ''yomeimon'' ({{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}})
==See also== * [[Mon (architecture)]] *The [[Glossary of Japanese Buddhism]] for an explanation of terms concerning Japanese Buddhism, Japanese Buddhist art, and Japanese Buddhist temple architecture.

{{short description|Zen Buddhist temple gate}} {{Italic title|reason=}} [[File:Tofukuji-Sanmon-M9589.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Tōfuku-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|WD=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] {{Main|Mon (architecture)}} A {{nihongo|'''''sanmon'''''|三門 or 山門}}, also called {{nihongo|'''''sangedatsumon'''''|三解脱門||lit. "gate of the three liberations"}}, is the most important gate of a Japanese Zen Buddhist temple, and is part of the Zen ''[[shichidō garan]]'', the group of buildings that forms the heart of a Zen Buddhist temple.<ref name="jaanus">JAANUS</ref> It can be often found in temples of other denominations too. Most ''sanmon'' are 2- or 3-{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=bay|1=Ken (unit)}} ''{{ill|Nijūmon|en|Nijūmon|WD=Q3341490|lt=Nijūmon|1=Nijūmon}}'' (a type of two-storied gate), but the name by itself does not imply any specific architecture.
==Position, function and structure== {{seealso|Shanmen}} [[File:Komyoji Winter.jpg|left|thumb|A ''sanmon'' seen through its ''sōmon'', (outer gate)]]Its importance notwithstanding, the ''sanmon'' is not the first gate of the temple, and in fact it usually stands between the ''{{ill|Sōmon|en|Sōmon|WD=Q3511697|lt=Sōmon|1=Sōmon}}'' (outer gate) and the ''{{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}'' (lit. "Hall of Buddha", i.e. the {{ill|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|en|Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)|WD=Q17051413|lt=main hall|1=Main Hall (Japanese Buddhism)}}). It used to be connected to a portico-like structure called {{nihongo|''{{ill|Kairō|en|Kairō|WD=Q7884494|lt=Kairō|1=Kairō}}''|廻廊}}, which however gradually disappeared during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, being replaced by the {{nihongo|''sanrō''|山廊}}, a small building present on both sides of the gate and containing a stairway to the gate's second story.<ref name="INJ"/> (Both ''sanrō'' are clearly visible in [[Tōfuku-ji]]'s photo above.)
The ''sanmon's'' size is an indicator of a Zen temple's status.<ref name="jaanus"/> Structurally, the ''sanmon'' of a first Rank temple as [[Nanzen-ji]] in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} is a two-storied, 5x2 bay,<ref>The space between two pillars, {{nihongo|''{{ill|Ken (unit)|en|Ken (unit)|WD=Q3195007|lt=ken|1=Ken (unit)}}''|間}} in Japanese</ref> three entrance gate (see photo below).<ref name="jaanus"/> Its three gates are called {{nihongo|''kūmon''|空門|gate of emptiness}}, {{nihongo|''musōmon''|無相門|gate of formlessness}} and {{nihongo|''muganmon''|無願門|gate of inaction}} and symbolize the three gates to enlightenment, or ''{{ill|Satori|en|Satori|WD=Q584580|lt=Satori|1=Satori}}''.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="fowler">Fowler</ref> Entering, pilgrims can symbolically free themselves from the three passions of {{nihongo|''ton''|貪|greed}}, {{nihongo|''shin''|瞋|hatred}}, and {{nihongo|''chi''|癡|foolishness}}.<ref>[http://www.zojoji.or.jp/en/index.html Zōjō-ji] accessed on May 1, 2009</ref> The fact the gate has entrances but no doors, and cannot therefore be closed, emphasizes its purely symbolic function as a limit between the sacred and the profane.
A temple of the second Rank will have a two-storied, 3x2-bay, single entrance gate (see photo below). The second story of a first or second Rank temple usually contains statues of [[Shakyamuni Tathāgata]] or of goddess [[Kannon]], and of the 16 [[:en:Arhats|''Rakan'']], and hosts periodical religious ceremonies.<ref name="jaanus"/><ref name="INJ">Iwanami Nihonshi Jiten</ref> The side bays of ''sanmon'' of the first two Ranks may also house statues of the {{ill|Nio (Buddhism)|en|Nio (Buddhism)|WD=Q539715|lt=Nio (Buddhism)|1=Nio (Buddhism)}}, wardens who are in charge of repelling evil.<ref name="jaanus"/>
A third Rank temple will have a single-storied, lx2-bay, single entrance gate.<ref name="jaanus"/>
===The three Ranks of a ''sanmon''=== <gallery>
Image:Myotsuji Sanmon 1.jpg| A middle Rank, three-bay ''sanmon'' at Myōtsū-ji, [[:en:Fukui prefecture|Fukui prefecture]] Image:Sozenji (Higashiyodogawa, Osaka) sanmon.jpg| A low Rank ''sanmon'' at Sozen-ji in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|WD=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} </gallery>
===The second story of a ''sanmon''=== Some images of the second story of {{ill|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|en|Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)|WD=Q3076914|lt=Kōmyō-ji|1=Kōmyō-ji (Kamakura)}}'s ''sanmon'' in {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, {{ill|Kanagawa Prefecture|en|Kanagawa Prefecture|WD=Q127513|lt=Kanagawa Prefecture|1=Kanagawa Prefecture}}. It is a high Rank [[Jōdo-shū|Jōdo]] sect ''sanmon'', the largest of the [[:en:Kantō|Kantō]] region. <gallery> File:Komyoji,-Kamakura gate stairs.jpg|The stairs to the second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside The Sanmon 3.jpg|The second story File:Kamakura Komyoji Sanmon 2.jpg|The second story, exit to the balcony File:Kamakura Komyoji Inside-the-sanmon-1.jpg|Sacred images in the main room </gallery>
==Major ''sanmon''== [[File:Chionin35n3200.jpg|thumb|275px|[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' (Japan's National Treasure)]] ;Case 1 *[[Chion-in]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) – The most important ''sanmon'' in Japan. *[[Nanzen-ji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}) *[[:en:Kuonji|Kuonji]]'s ''sanmon'' ({{ill|Minobu, Yamanashi|en|Minobu, Yamanashi|WD=Q1204435|lt=Minobu|1=Minobu, Yamanashi}})
;Case 2 *[[Tōdai-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|WD=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}) *[[Hōryū-ji]]'s ''nandaimon'' ({{ill|Ikaruga, Nara|en|Ikaruga, Nara|WD=Q379233|lt=Ikaruga|1=Ikaruga, Nara}}) *{{ill|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|en|Nikkō Tōshō-gū|WD=Q696641|lt=Tōshō-gū|1=Nikkō Tōshō-gū}}'s ''yomeimon'' ({{ill|Nikkō|en|Nikkō|WD=Q235753|lt=Nikkō|1=Nikkō}})
